TextENGNew: The next day, Rena came to school just as she'd promised—acting perfectly normal, as though nothing were amiss.
Watching her chat with Mion so naturally and cheerfully, I almost began to wonder if everything I'd heard last night had been a lie.
That's how utterly outlandish the story had been.

That an unthinkable endemic disease was rampant in this village, and that a shadowy group of religious fanatics was plotting to exploit it to revive their faith.@k ...And that they were researching the pathogen and planning to unleash it on the village...

Last night I'd been completely swept up by Rena's intensity and had swallowed that preposterous story whole,@k ...but thinking about it calmly in the light of day, it was impossible to take it seriously.
Everything Rena told me was just a rehash of that dubious Takano-san's ideas. It was entirely hypothetical—plausible-sounding claims without a single shred of evidence.
...Granted, the fact that Takano-san herself had become a victim of the incidents did lend it a certain credibility, but...
I looked at Mion.
First thing in the morning, and she was already playfully pestering Rena.
changeReason: Old drops the time reference 朝っぱらから (first thing in the morning) present in JPN; new preserves it, adding temporal context.

TextENGNew: ...I simply couldn't imagine Mion being involved in some outrageous conspiracy.
``Hey, you're not listening, are you, Kei-chan?``
``Huh?!@k@vS09/01/170100719. Oh... ah... sorry.@k@vS09/01/170100720. I wasn't paying attention.``
``Aha ha ha ha!``
``Still half asleep, are we?@k@vS09/03/170300310. If you don't get enough sleep, that brain of yours stops working, you know!``
Mion teased me for being so slow to react, lost in thought as I was.
...Could anyone really distrust this vivacious, cheerful girl?
I trust Mion.@k I trust my friends.
My friends would never be complicit in a conspiracy that absurd.
If she knew about something like that, there's no way she could act this carefree.
...Rena had insisted it was too dangerous to consult Mion, but...
Wouldn't it be worth confiding in Mion behind Rena's back?
...Of course. There was perhaps a one percent chance that the conspiracy was real, that Mion was the enemy, and that I'd be walking right into a trap.
But if asked whether I'd believe that outlandish conspiracy theory or trust my friend, the answer was obvious.
My friend Mion would absolutely, positively never take part in any such conspiracy.
Even if the conspiracy truly existed, Mion would still be on our side. I was sure of it.
So whether or not the conspiracy was real, it had to be worth telling Mion...
``What is it, Kei-chan?@k@vS09/03/170300312. Why'd you call me out here?``
``And telling me to come alone, no less.@k@vS09/03/170300314. You're not about to shake me down for lunch money, are you?``
``Come on, is that really the first thing you think when someone calls you behind the gym storeroom...?``
``Then what, a love letter?@k@vS09/03/170300316. But isn't that usually done through the shoe lockers?``
``In the city, not necessarily.@k@vS09/01/170100723. Plenty of guys just hand it over face-to-face.``
``...As if.@k@vS09/01/170100725. Look, I need to talk to you about something serious.@k@vS09/01/170100726. No joking around—just hear me out.``
``...What... what is it...?``
``There are people trying to revive the Oyashiro-sama faith...... You know about that?``
``...Huh?@k@vS09/03/170300321. What are you talking about, Kei-chan?``
This was, of course, a test on my part.
I wanted to throw a fastball and see how Mion reacted.
But Mion looked genuinely, truly bewildered.
...When someone hits a nerve, they can't stay this composed.@k There would always be some small reaction.
Unless Mion were a genius-level actress... it genuinely seemed as though the concept of an Oyashiro-sama revival meant nothing to her.
...I decided to lay it all on the table.

``Mion.@k@vS09/01/170100729. Your family, the Sonozaki family, has enormous influence over this area, right?@k@vS09/01/170100730. ...Could they be working behind the scenes to revive the Oyashiro-sama faith?``
``...Revive?@k@vS09/03/170300323. Why would they?``
``Well, you know.@k@vS09/01/170100732. The current Oyashiro-sama faith is way weaker than it was in the ancient days, right?``
``What if there were people—fundamentalist types—trying to drag it back to the level of old Onigafuchi, pulling strings from the shadows?``
``What?@k@vS09/03/170300325. Wait, wait, wait.@k@vS09/03/170300326. What are you even talking about?``
``...You really... have no idea what I'm talking about...?``
``Of course I don't!@k@vS09/03/170300328. What is this, some kind of bestselling thriller plot?``
Mion's reaction was completely natural.
There was no way she was pretending ignorance while actually hiding the truth.@k ...But I pressed on regardless.
``Look, I'm telling you...@k@vS09/01/170100736. There really are people like that in this village.@k@vS09/01/170100737. You don't know anything about them?``
``Reviving the Oyashiro-sama faith to Onigafuchi levels...?@k@vS09/03/170300330. What would anyone stand to gain from that?``
``...Well... I'd imagine it means a lot to devout, fanatical believers...``

``But Kei-chan,@k@vS09/03/170300332. who'd be happy to go back to those harsh ancient rules?@k@vS09/03/170300333. In this day and age, telling people they can't leave the village? You couldn't even make a living!``

``B-But for example, look... Rika-chan!@k@vS09/01/170100740. Everyone says she's the reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama, and she gets fawned over, right?@k@vS09/01/170100741. What about the people around her? Her inner circle of fans, or whatever?``
changeReason: Old says 'those people might be religious fanatics'; new says 'what about the people around her? Her inner circle?' JPN mentions 取り巻きの老人たち (surrounding elderly) and 執着的な一派 (obsessive faction) -- different framing of suspicion.

TextENGNew: ``Rika-chan's inner circle? You mean like old man Kimiyoshi?``
``I mean... sure, he's devout, but I wouldn't call him a fanatic.``
``I'm telling you, they exist!@k@vS09/01/170100743. People who are trying to bring back the original Oyashiro-sama faith!``
``Ah ha ha ha ha!``
``...Fine, fine.@k@vS09/03/170300338. I'll play along for now so you can finish your story.@k@vS09/03/170300339. So what then?``
``So, these people discovered that the true nature of Oyashiro-sama is an endemic disease unique to this area, and they're researching the pathogen.``
``Where?``
``And what's an endemic disease?@k@vS09/03/170300342. What's a pathogen?@k@vS09/03/170300343. Eew, cooties.``
...Even I was getting confused trying to explain this...
Last night, I'd been completely swept up by Rena and had swallowed that preposterous tale,@k ...but now, trying to put it into my own words, I could see just how incoherent it really was...
``What's gotten into you?@k@vS09/03/170300345. Did Miyo-san fill your head with weird ideas?``

``She's famous for this.@k@vS09/03/170300347. She's an occult maniac—always concocting outrageous fantasy stories.@k@vS09/03/170300348. And every now and then, someone actually takes her seriously, which makes it even more delicious for her.``
``She told me one of her stories once too, and honestly, it was pretty entertaining.@k@vS09/03/170300350. Aha ha ha.``
``...Is that... really true?``
``You've probably heard bits and pieces yourself, Kei-chan, but this village did have a somewhat unusual culture way back when.``

``It had some rather intense elements—strict laws, harsh punishments, cruel customs. Apparently, it's fairly well-known among occult enthusiasts.@k@vS09/03/170300353. Miyo-san is one of those enthusiasts, and this village is her personal playground.``
``Then... the story about the village being controlled by some strange parasite is...``
``...Is that her latest one?``
``What do you mean, 'latest one'?``
``I told you, Miyo-san is an occult maniac.@k@vS09/03/170300358. She studies Onigafuchi's bizarre legends and entertains herself by cooking up all sorts of hypotheses, deductions, and fantasies.``
``So the parasite theory is just one of many. She's come up with all kinds of others.@k@vS09/03/170300360. The one I heard ages ago was the UFO theory.@k@vS09/03/170300361. That one was wild.``
changeReason: Old says 'interesting, since it connected a lot of different dots' matching JPN 辻褄があってて面白い (logically consistent and interesting); new replaces with 'That one was wild', losing the coherence detail.

TextENGNew: ``In ancient times, a UFO crashed into Onigafuchi Swamp, and aliens emerged from it and fought the villagers... That kind of B-movie sci-fi horror setup.``

``The one Satoko heard was the underground people theory, I think.@k@vS09/03/170300363. Tectonic shifts created a connection between an underground kingdom and Onigafuchi, and then something about...``
changeReason: Old says 'creatures from the bottom of the ocean' but 地底人 means 'underground people' -- wrong location entirely.

TextENGNew: ``...Right, right—a princess from the underground kingdom fell in love with a young villager, and they reconciled their peoples. A sci-fi Romeo and Juliet.``
``And it sounds like her latest creation is the parasite theory you heard, Kei-chan.@k@vS09/03/170300366. Honestly, she should collect them all into a book someday, heh heh heh!``
Mion laughed so hard she had to twist and hold her stomach.
...Watching her laugh like that... I finally realized what a ridiculous story I'd taken at face value.
There was no doubt about it anymore.
...I had been completely taken in by a preposterous tale.
``Sorry, Mion!@k@vS09/01/170100751. Just answer my questions!@k@vS09/01/170100752. First... do people trying to revive the Oyashiro-sama faith actually exist?!``

``They don't.@k@vS09/03/170300368. If anything, it's the exact opposite.@k@vS09/03/170300369. The Three Families have already agreed that Hinamizawa should open itself up to the outside and prosper.``

``This is still a secret, but there's a plan to build a new expressway through this area, with the target completion set for the twenty-first century.@k@vS09/03/170300371. The Sonozaki family is currently working behind the scenes to attract the route here.``
changeReason: Old says '20th century' but Japanese ２１世紀 means '21st century' -- wrong century.

TextENGNew: ``If it works out, this whole area will boom overnight.@k@vS09/03/170300373. My grandma's betting that the value of the mountain forests will skyrocket and she'll make a killing.``
changeReason: Old says 'land prices in the region'; new says 'value of the mountain forests' matching JPN 山林の地価 (mountain forest land prices). Different asset specificity.

TextENGNew: ``Well, it's a twenty-first-century project, so it'll probably fall to me as the big job I inherit.``
``So then... people who despise outsiders... they don't exist?``
``I keep telling you, they don't!``
``Why would we want to make this village even more inconvenient than it already is?``
``In fact, the village is already being marketed as a vacation home area.@k@vS09/03/170300378. Isn't your house literally the very first one, Kei-chan?``
``The villagers all welcomed you, didn't they?@k@vS09/03/170300380. The Three Families are working hard to stop the outflow of young people, you know.``
There wasn't the slightest cloud in what Mion was saying.
It carried an authenticity that Rena's parroting of Takano-san's delusions couldn't begin to match.
...Mion's words were opening my eyes, one by one.
``Th-Then what about this? I heard that the evidence of parasite infection is some kind of blood count that's abnormally high only in Hinamizawa residents...``
``Is that so?@k@vS09/03/170300382. And how exactly did Miyo-san go about investigating that?``
``Well, Takano-san's a nurse, so if she wanted to look into it, she could...``
``And who's going to cooperate with blood draws for some shady study like that?``
``Nobody, that's who.@k@vS09/03/170300385. And even if she did draw blood, where and how did she analyze it?@k@vS09/03/170300386. How many samples could Miyo-san possibly have examined on her own?``
...Mion was right.
I'd let Takano-san's profession as a nurse fill my head with overblown assumptions.
A public health center conducting a large-scale blood survey would be one thing, but how much investigating could Takano-san realistically do on her own?
changeReason: Old misses the comparative structure from JPN: 保健所なんかが大々的に...というならともかく (if a public health center did it, that would be one thing). New accurately captures this rhetorical contrast.

TextENGNew: ...When I thought about it logically... this clearly wasn't the kind of story you could take at face value.
``Um, I also heard that the proof of parasite infection is that people here don't get hay fever...!``

``Isn't hay fever a city thing?@k@vS09/03/170300388. I thought it had something to do with exhaust fumes.@k@vS09/03/170300389. This is Hinamizawa, Kei-chan.@k@vS09/03/170300390. Out here in the countryside with all this clean air, of course nobody gets hay fever.``
changeReason: Old specifically mentions Okinomiya (興宮) as referenced in JPN; new replaces it with a general 'countryside' reference, omitting the place name.

TextENGNew: ``Well... then what about this?!@k@vS09/01/170100758. The Sonozaki family has an assassination squad under the direct command of the head of the main house, and they were behind that kidnapping of the minister's grandson!``
changeReason: Old says 'has a death squad'; new says 'assassination squad under the direct command of the head of the main house'. JPN 園崎本家頭首直属の暗殺部隊 specifies the chain of command, which new preserves.

TextENGNew: ``Ah ha ha ha ha!@k@vS09/03/170300392. Wouldn't that be something!@k@vS09/03/170300393. Aha ha ha ha!``
...Even I could hear how ridiculous I sounded.
...And yet, last night, I'd believed every word.
...Rena had been so earnest, so intense, that I'd swallowed it whole.

``It's true that the minister's grandson was suddenly discovered near Takatsudo.@k@vS09/03/170300395. But that wasn't us.@k@vS09/03/170300396. It wasn't us, but we acted as though it were our doing.``
``...That's confusing. Explain.``

``Sure.@k@vS09/03/170300398. During the dam war, the Onigafuchi Guardians did engage in some pretty dangerous resistance activities. That's true.@k@vS09/03/170300399. Including things that bordered on intimidation.``
changeReason: Old says 'something close to terrorism' but Japanese 脅迫めいた means 'resembling threats/intimidation.'

TextENGNew: ``But back then, there were also unsavory types who exploited the movement.@k@vS09/03/170300401. Imposters who used the Guardians' name to extort money and valuables.``
The Onigafuchi Guardians had been an extremely aggressive force at that time.
It's entirely plausible that opportunists saw a chance to use their name for extortion amid the chaos.
``The minister's grandson kidnapping was one of those.@k@vS09/03/170300403. The kidnappers probably impersonated the Guardians when demanding ransom to throw the police investigation off track.``

``Honestly, even the Guardians were shocked by that one!@k@vS09/03/170300405. People were buzzing about how they'd actually managed to kidnap a minister's grandson.@k@vS09/03/170300406. We all wished we could have met the culprits and shaken their hands!``
changeReason: Old says 'give them an award'; new says 'shaken their hands'. JPN 表彰してやろう means 'commend/give them an award' -- different gestures of admiration.

TextENGNew: ``W-Wait a second!@k@vS09/01/170100761. So the Onigafuchi Guardians had... nothing to do with the kidnapping?!``
``Nope.@k@vS09/03/170300408. Nothing at all.``
Mion said it without hesitation.
A clean, definitive answer—like splitting bamboo. Not the slightest waver.
And when you thought about it, it made perfect sense.
Kidnapping a minister's grandson and negotiating directly with a government minister—that's an unthinkably enormous crime.
No matter how aggressive the Guardians were, that kind of operation was clearly beyond what Hinamizawa residents could pull off.@k It was obviously the work of professional criminals!
``...But, like I started to say a moment ago.@k@vS09/03/170300410. The Sonozaki main house behaved as though they were the masterminds behind the kidnapping.``
``What do you mean, 'behaved'...?``
``...This is... a secret family precept of the Sonozaki main house.@k@vS09/03/170300412. I'll tell you in confidence, Kei-chan, but you absolutely cannot tell anyone, okay?!``
``The Sonozaki main house has a tradition of bluffing. Whenever some incident occurs that works to our advantage, we act as if we orchestrated it.``
changeReason: Old drops 'Sonozaki main house' (JPN 園崎本家) at the start, making the speaker's affiliation unclear. New correctly attributes the bluffing tradition to the specific family branch.

TextENGNew: ``...Bluffing...!@k@vS09/01/170100764. You mean... a bluff?!``

``Exactly.@k@vS09/03/170300415. If something benefits us, it doesn't even have to be a crime.@k@vS09/03/170300416. It could be the weather, or the winning lottery numbers.@k@vS09/03/170300417. If it works in our favor, we just say, 'Yes, that was us.'@k@vS09/03/170300418. That's the tradition.``
``And what happens?@k@vS09/03/170300419. It starts to feel like we're some incredibly powerful shadow organization, right?@k@vS09/03/170300420. That's exactly the point.``

``It's a secret image strategy passed down orally from one Sonozaki head to the next, generation after generation.@k@vS09/03/170300422. A strategy to make us look far more formidable than we actually are.``
``...Whoooa.........@k@vS09/01/170100766. Then... th-then what about the plan during the dam war to storm the Ministry of Construction?!@k@vS09/01/170100767. What about that?!``
``Heh heh heh heh!@k@vS09/03/170300424. Same thing exactly, Kei-chan.@k@vS09/03/170300425. Nobody would actually carry out something that absurd, right?``

``The whole point was to make people believe 'they might actually do it'—to create that fear and that illusion. That was the real objective.@k@vS09/03/170300427. In other words, yet another image strategy.``
changeReason: Old says 'exact pressure on the government'; new says 'make people believe they might do it, create fear and illusion'. JPN says プレッシャーと幻想 (pressure and illusion) to あいつら (those guys), not specifically government.

TextENGNew: The preparations and training were all meant to apply pressure. The plan was never intended to be executed.@k ...The Sonozaki bluff.
Of course.@k Of course...!
Occupying the Ministry of Construction was obviously impossible—and even if they could, it wouldn't solve anything. A moment's thought would tell you that!
Even if they managed to occupy it, the police would swarm in and crush them in no time!
changeReason: Old adds 'Why didn't I think of that?' not in the Japanese.

TextENGNew: ``So the weapons and the training were...``
``As for the weapons... ahaha, I'll plead the fifth on that one.@k@vS09/03/170300429. But we did train. Properly and thoroughly, so the bluff would be convincing!``

``With Kasai-san as leader, we took a bunch of the younger guys from my father's organization out to the Texas desert to play war games with trainers, and afterwards we leisurely sightseered around San Francisco.@k@vS09/03/170300432. It was practically a vacation!``
I couldn't think of anything else to say.@k ...It was obvious now.
I—no, Rena too.
...We'd been completely played by Takano-san's delusional scrapbooks...
Rena had been so deadly serious about it that I'd let myself get swept up... but looking at it now, it was riddled with contradictions.
What I found even harder to believe was why Rena had taken such a delusion at face value.
...But I knew the reason.
...The shock of the day we'd discovered the bodies had left her in a fragile state.
And then an intoxicating fantasy had been skillfully planted in that vulnerable mind... and she'd swallowed it.
changeReason: Old says 'came across this tall tale and believed it' (accidental); new says 'skillfully planted in that vulnerable mind' (deliberate manipulation). JPN 巧みに吹き込まれ means 'skillfully instilled', indicating deliberate action.

TextENGNew: Rena is a smart person.@k Under normal circumstances, she'd never take a tall tale like this seriously!
And then another sobering revelation followed.
The white van I'd seen yesterday appeared during the lunch break, and the four men in work uniforms came to the school.
I thanked Mion and quietly followed the men.
The leader-looking man who'd spoken to me yesterday was visiting the teachers' office.
``Howdy! We're from Okonogi Gardeners!``
``Oh, thank you for coming.@k@vS09/22/171400010. It's another hot one today, isn't it?``
``Oh, not at all! And you're looking lovely as ever today, teacher! Ueh heh heh!``
``Well then, I'll leave you to it. Thank you again.``
changeReason: Old says 'you can start whenever you're ready' (instruction to begin); new says 'I'll leave you to it' (departure). Different actions from the speaker.

TextENGNew: ``Right-o, we'll get started then.@k@vS09/14/173400017. Our office manager asked me to hand this over to you.``

``The estimate and invoice, I see.@k@vS09/22/171400013. Thank you.@k@vS09/22/171400014. The date is left blank, I see—no problem.@k@vS09/22/171400015. Then I'll ask the principal to deliver these later.``
``Right then, we'll get to work!``
The man had been chatting with Chie-sensei in an extremely friendly manner.
``...Hm?@k@vS09/00/173200002. What are you doing here, Maebara-kun?``
``Oh, Principal...@k@vS09/01/170100772. Um, who were those men just now...?``
The principal pointed toward the window.@k When I looked outside, I heard a 'BRRRRRMM!' like an engine roaring to life.
It was the sound of a brush cutter—a motorized trimmer with a rotary blade—starting up.
changeReason: Old says 'motorized lawn mower' but JPN 回転ノコギリの付いた草刈機 describes a brush cutter with a rotary blade; new accurately describes the equipment.

TextENGNew: I could see the young men in work clothes unloading hedge trimmers, bamboo brooms, and a ladder from the van.
changeReason: Equipment changes from 'lawn mower' to 'brush cutter/trimmer', which is a different tool.

TextENGNew: ``They're a gardening company.@k@vS09/00/173200004. The hedges and trees had gotten quite overgrown.@k@vS09/00/173200005. I thought we should have them pruned before summer.``
changeReason: Old says 'grass around the fence' but JPN 垣根 means hedges/hedgerows; old says 'clean them up' but JPN 剪定 specifically means pruning. New is more precise.

TextENGNew: ``Mr. Principal! Thank you kindly for the business!``
Noticing the principal, the man removed his cap and bowed.
``Indeed!@k@vS09/00/173200007. I'm counting on you.``
changeReason: Old says 'No need! I trust your work' which is invented; Japanese simply says 'Indeed! Please take good care of it.'

TextENGNew: ``We'll try to wrap it up today!@k@vS09/14/173400021. It'll be a bit noisy for a while—hope you'll bear with us!``
Each of the four workers grabbed their tools and headed for the overgrown hedges.
The engine noise from the brush cutters was quite loud, and the children playing in the schoolyard watched with fascination.
changeReason: Equipment changes from 'lawn mower' to 'brush cutter/trimmer', which is a different tool.

TextENGNew: ``Hey, everyone!@k@vS09/22/171400017. It's dangerous out here—go back to your classrooms!``
That was Chie-sensei.
She handed the document envelope the man had given her to the principal.
Printed on the envelope in green letters were the words 'Okonogi Gardeners, Inc.'
``Principal,@k@vS09/22/171400019. I'm sorry to trouble you, but could you bring this invoice along the next time you visit city hall?``
``Certainly.@k@vS09/00/173200009. Understood.``
``Um... Chie-sensei.@k@vS09/01/170100774. Those gardeners... did they come by yesterday too?``
``Yes.@k@vS09/22/171400021. They came by yesterday evening for a preliminary look.@k@vS09/22/171400022. They mentioned getting lost while trying to take a shortcut on the way back.``
``The narrow roads in this village are quite a maze.@k@vS09/00/173200011. Can't blame them at all.``
'That's right, when I first came here too...' Chie-sensei chimed in with her own story, and the teachers shared a good laugh.
There had been... nothing suspicious from the very start.
...Yes, the reason I'd found those men suspicious yesterday was entirely because Rena's story had gotten to me.
Hadn't I been thinking it myself at the time? White vans aren't the least bit unusual!
It was just that I'd happened to see one parked there right after hearing that creepy story, and the timing had spooked me.
The gardeners, unfamiliar with Hinamizawa's roads, had simply turned down an odd side road on their way home.
That was all.
...What a colossal misunderstanding...!
I'd believed that flimsy story about the Sonozaki family pulling strings behind the village, let my imagination run wild conjuring up cabals of fanatics.@k I'd even suspected Mion, of all people!
Damn it!!@k What kind of fool am I, Keiichi Maebara!@k She's my friend!
We joined hands that day and declared that friends are family, that we're unconditionally allies—didn't we?!
And I was the one who said it first, yet I went and doubted my own friend... I was completely out of my mind...!!
...But more importantly, Rena!
How long was she going to keep believing this outrageous story?!
I had to snap her out of it, fast!
Unlike her usual self, the current Rena seemed to have no room left in her heart.
Right now, she genuinely, truly believed that the Sonozaki family was the mastermind behind an action-movie-level conspiracy...!
I had firmly memorized the van's license plate, and I clearly remembered each of their faces.
...No mistake—this was one of the vans that had been monitoring me!
So far, I'd confirmed approximately three or four vehicles surveilling my movements.
This white van had been the most persistent of them all, so it was the one I remembered best.
The others were most likely just performing surveillance, while this van had to be tasked with direct actions like abduction and assassination.
The other cars secure the perimeter, and then the strike team's vehicle swoops in...!
That's their standard method for kidnappings and assassinations—it was written in the scrapbooks.
...If that were true, the fact that these people had come right onto school grounds was no laughing matter.
Surely they wouldn't attack me openly on campus.
The enemy was in a covert phase, thoroughly concealing their fangs and claws until the moment of their terrifying ultimate objective.
...So they couldn't act here at school, where there were too many eyes...!
But just now, those men had been chatting intimately with the teachers.
changeReason: Old says 'talking with Chie-sensei' (specific teacher); new says 'chatting with the teachers' (plural). JPN 先生たち is plural -- different scope of who they were interacting with.

TextENGNew: I'd only caught snatches of their conversation, but no matter how I looked at it, it was suspicious.
I desperately tried to recall the fragments of voice I'd overheard, the movements of their lips, even their breathing—playing it back like a video tape in my mind.
``Oh, thank you for coming.@k@vS09/22/171400024. It's very hot today, isn't it?``
``Oh, not at all... you look ...........................today...! Ueh heh heh!``
``Thank you. Well, you can start whenever you're ready.``
``Right-o, we'll get started.@k@vS09/14/173400025. Our office manager told me to hand this over.``
``The ...... and ......, I see.@k@vS09/22/171400027. Thank you.@k@vS09/22/171400028. ...is blank, I see—no problem.@k@vS09/22/171400029. Then I'll ask the principal to deliver these.``
``Right then, we'll get to ...............!``
I used what I knew to deduce the situation. I replayed the video in my head over and over, sometimes in slow motion, sometimes freezing it to study each detail.
...And then, something horrifying—something I could scarcely believe—began to surface.
That the men and Chie-sensei were acquainted was beyond question.
...And yet, I'd never seen these men visit the school before. There should have been no connection.
There was no doubt that these men were an assassination squad directly under the Sonozaki main house.
That fluent local accent was proof that they were Sonozaki-gumi men, hand-picked from among their ranks as a special ops team.
The assassination squad's interaction with Chie-sensei.@k Acquaintance.@k Connection.
Come to think of it, what kind of person was Chie-sensei, really?
During the dam war, the Hinamizawa school was slated for closure and merger with the school in Okinomiya.
...But the Onigafuchi Guardians, refusing to accept the closure, had seized part of the forestry office and unilaterally converted it into a school.
A self-proclaimed school with no actual teacher, naturally.
It was meant to be nothing more than a token gesture of resistance.@k ...But then, a passionate young teacher, moved by the 'plight' of those students, rushed to join them.
That was Rumiko Chie-sensei.
...It was obviously unnatural. Teachers are public servants employed by the prefecture.
As long as they keep their heads down and avoid making waves, they won't be fired or reassigned.
They're guaranteed employment until retirement, with a fixed pension waiting at the end.
And yet this public servant had defied the Board of Education to come to Hinamizawa.
The village today hails her as a 'teacher of passion.'
...The story goes that when Chie-sensei was suspended for defying the Board of Education's orders, Oryou Sonozaki herself personally negotiated to protect her position—and won.
...In other words, connect the scattered dots by the shortest path and the answer is simple.
Chie-sensei had been recruited by Oryou Sonozaki as a teacher for the 'school' the Onigafuchi Guardians had fabricated.
That is—she'd been under Oryou Sonozaki's influence even before she joined this school!
As for the principal, he'd originally been this school's principal from the start.
As a community leader, he had more than ample ties to the Three Families.@k In other words, in other words, in other words...
changeReason: Old says 'So, that means...'; new preserves JPN triple repetition つまりつまりつまり as 'In other words, in other words, in other words...' conveying the speaker's agitation.

TextENGNew: This school had been under Sonozaki surveillance from the very beginning!
So naturally, the assassination squad and Chie-sensei would be acquainted.
...Had Chie-sensei confirmed my attendance today, then made the call... and summoned them here...?!
...I didn't want to think about why she'd called them.
If mere surveillance were the goal, they could just wait near the gate after school and tail me.
Why go to the trouble of coming onto school grounds...?
Stop playing dumb, Rena Ryuugu!
Do you think pretending not to notice will make it not happen?!
Think, think, and stay cool, Rena Ryuugu!!
They're planning to make their move on me right here at school—today!
At school?!
With all my classmates and friends around?! How?!
Simple.
All it would take is the teacher announcing during final homeroom: 'Ryuugu-san, I need to see you in the teachers' office.'
What about my club friends?
She's a teacher—she could easily come up with some excuse to shoo them away.
The classroom has interior work today, so everyone needs to head straight home after school—any excuse like that would do!
``Oh, not at all... you look ...........................today...! Ueh heh heh!``
``Thank you. Well, you can start whenever you're ready.``
...There's no mistake...
They couldn't have said 'they're ready to proceed today' more plainly!!
And then... what else was there?
``Right-o, we'll get started.@k@vS09/14/173400029. Our office manager told me to hand this over.``
``The ...... and ......, I see.@k@vS09/22/171400032. Thank you.@k@vS09/22/171400033. ...is blank, I see—no problem.@k@vS09/22/171400034. Then I'll ask the principal to deliver these.``
That's right.@k He'd handed Chie-sensei an envelope containing some sort of documents.
...The word 'blank' carried an ominous air.
...Shouldn't my name have been written there...?
And the fact that it was left 'blank'—what did that mean?
I'm telling you, Rena Ryuugu!
Stop with this magical thinking that pretending not to notice will keep bad things from happening!!
That's exactly what it means.
This is an order from the higher-ups to make 'Rena Ryuugu' become 'blank'!!
Peeking from the shadows, I saw the principal had taken documents from the envelope and was reading through them.
His expression was stern... his eyes scanning the contents with deep seriousness.
...I wished I could get close enough to read what was written, but that was impossible.
Hoping for even the smallest clue, I stared intently at the scene... and then the envelope caught my eye.
At the bottom of the envelope, 'Okonogi Gardeners, Inc.' was printed, along with what appeared to be a phone number.
Fortunately, I have good eyesight.
...I quickly committed the number to memory.
If those men were an assassination squad, this gardening company was just a front.@k It probably didn't even exist.
I went searching for a phone to confirm my suspicion.
...The only one was in the teachers' office.
Fortunately, both teachers were out in the hallway, chatting while watching the gardeners work.@k Now was my chance.
Making sure no one saw me enter the teachers' office, I rushed in and grabbed the phone on the principal's desk.
I repeated the phone number in my head and punched it in all at once.
...In moments like these, I was grateful for a push-button phone.@k A rotary dial would have been agonizing.
If someone answered, I'd simply ask, 'Is this Okonogi Gardeners?'
All I needed was a yes or no before hanging up. Simple.
It connected!
``...The number you have dialed is...``
I could feel frost crystallizing up from the soles of my feet—a brutal, violent cold.
Wait—don't jump to conclusions...
Maybe I'd gotten the number wrong?
...No, I had it right.
It was an easy number to remember, almost catchy.@k Impossible to get wrong.@k Then had I mispressed a button?
I hung up, then dialed the number again, this time more deliberately, more slowly.
With the teachers potentially returning at any moment, going slowly and carefully bordered on suicidal.@k But... this was something I had to verify, right now, beyond any doubt...!
``...The number you have dialed is...``
Just as I feared... the same message.
...There was no longer any doubt.
'Okonogi Gardeners' didn't exist as a company.
It was a front for the assassination squad that served the dark underbelly of the Sonozaki family!!
``...Please check the number and try your call again.@k@vS09/00/tel17001. ...The number you have dialed is...``
While I stood there stunned, the emotionless automated voice droned on and on—making my skin crawl.
I hung up the phone and hurried out of the teachers' office.
Then I dashed into a bathroom stall, clutching my head in both hands, trying to suppress the turmoil in my mind.
Calm down, Rena.@k Calm down, Rena Ryuugu.@k Stay cool.@k Rena Ryuugu...!
It meant this school was no longer safe.
If I stayed here, I would be openly eliminated.
Eliminated?@k But why?!@k They couldn't be certain yet whether I had the scrapbooks...!
My head, stewing in a broth of suspicion, was like a boiling soup pot.
I kept chopping up clues and information, facts and suspicions like vegetables and tossing them in.
They bobbed and sank and foamed with scum as they boiled away...
...No, that can't be right. Keiichi-kun is my ally.@k Keiichi-kun alone is on my side.@k ...The hand he reached out to me at that garbage heap was real.
And the strength with which he'd pulled me close was unmistakable—there was nothing in it but a pure, wholehearted resolve to be on my side...!
So Keiichi-kun is different. I must not doubt Keiichi-kun. Keiichi-kun alone I must trust. Keiichi-kun alone is my ally now...!!
``...I'm sorry, I'm sorry... Keiichi-kun...@k@vS09/02/170201006. I almost doubted you... I'm so sorry, truly, I'm so sorry...``
I deliberately spoke the words aloud, repenting for having doubted Keiichi-kun—the one person who should have been my unconditional ally.
...Come to think of it, whether or not I had the scrapbooks might not even matter to them.
If someone looks suspicious, eliminate them!
That would be the safest approach.
...Which would mean... they're getting desperate?
'The Day of Oyashiro-sama's Revival' is drawing near...@k and they want to remove any thorns in their side now, by any means necessary?
There's no need to let her run free anymore.@k ...If you're that worried, just get rid of her...
Those were exactly the kind of words Oryou Sonozaki would say.
...Would say?
No—she DID say them!
That's why they're here today!!
Then what do I do? What am I supposed to do?!
Sit here and wait calmly until school lets out?!
Right now I was a goldfish in a tank.
No matter how many times I slipped past the net, this tank belonged to them in the first place—there was no point!
Then what should I do?!
It wasn't the net I needed to escape—it was the tank itself!

...I didn't know how to fight them. I didn't know who was on my side. I was in the grip of absolute despair.@k ...But at the very least, I needed to think about surviving right now, if only to buy time to plan my next move.
Escape. I have to escape this tank...!
When I stepped out of the bathroom, a female classmate was just about to go in.
I feigned illness, bracing myself against the wall as I spoke to her.
``I'm sorry... I'm really not feeling well...@k@vS09/02/170201008. Could you let the teacher know I'm going home early...?``
If escape was the goal, obviously I shouldn't leave word that I was leaving.
But if I didn't, they might interpret it as me fleeing in a panic.
If that happened, they would pursue me without restraint.@k And then I wouldn't stand a chance!
They'd been planning to capture me after school, but I 'happened' to leave early because I felt sick—I just slipped through their fingers.
...If they saw it that way, they wouldn't suspect that I'd noticed their net closing around me.
I started toward the classroom to get my bag, then immediately stopped and changed direction for the entrance.
I didn't need my bag anymore.
When I realized no one was watching, I dropped the pretense of illness and broke into a run.
``...Hmm?@k@vS09/00/173200013. Chie-sensei, something seems off here.``
``Off?@k@vS09/22/171400036. What seems to be the problem?``
``Indeed.@k@vS09/00/173200015. The dates on both the estimate and the invoice are left blank.@k@vS09/00/173200016. Shouldn't today's date go there?``

``Ah, that's apparently some kind of administrative requirement.@k@vS09/22/171400038. They said having the same date on the estimate and invoice causes issues.@k@vS09/22/171400039. The clerk told me to leave the dates blank and let them fill them in later.``
``I see.@k@vS09/00/173200018. And another thing—the phone number on the invoice is different from the one on the envelope.@k@vS09/00/173200019. Which one is correct?``
``Oh my, you're right.@k@vS09/22/171400041. ...Excuse me, sir!``
``Yes?@k@vS09/14/173400031. What can I do for you?``
``The phone number on the invoice doesn't match the one on the envelope. Which is correct?``
``Oh!!@k@vS09/14/173400033. Aha ha ha ha!@k@vS09/14/173400034. The one on the invoice is the right number, so no worries!@k@vS09/14/173400035. We just changed our phone number, you see!``

``Sorry about that.@k@vS09/14/173400037. I told our part-timer to stamp all the envelopes with the new number, but I guess she missed some.@k@vS09/14/173400038. Sorry, sorry—just cross out the old one and it'll be fine!``
